item.text: The finding points at Vert.x TLS SNI context selection. I’m reading those classes and their tests now so the fix matches the existing cache/provider model.

The risky behavior is likely cache key collapse: provider cache keys currently ignore hostname verification, client-auth mode, ALPN/application protocols, cipher/protocol sets, and CRL paths. That can reuse a context built with different certificate-validation settings.

I’m going to split the manager’s cache keys: the lower-level key will cover certificate material and CRLs, while the provider key will also cover validation and TLS negotiation settings. That keeps existing reuse where it is safe, and prevents reusing a provider with stale certificate-validation state.

item.text: I’m doing one more pass on adjacent TLS trust code to make sure the finding is not about an explicit trust-all path. `trustAll` is an intentional option here; the remediation stays focused on accidental reuse of insecure validation settings.

One issue surfaced in the review: Java `Set.equals` ignores order, but this code preserves cipher/protocol ordering elsewhere. I’m tightening the provider key to snapshot those ordered sets as lists so two configurations with the same entries in a different order do not collapse.
The ordered-key adjustment is done. I’m running lightweight local checks that do not need Maven dependency resolution.

Implemented the remediation.

Changed [SslContextManager.java](/work/vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/internal/tls/SslContextManager.java:51) so the `SslContextProvider` cache no longer keys only on cert/trust material. It now includes hostname verification, client auth, ALPN application protocols, cipher suite order, secure transport protocol order, and CRL paths/values. This prevents reusing a provider created with weaker or different certificate-validation settings.

Added regression coverage in [SslContextManagerTest.java](/work/vertx-core/src/test/java/io/vertx/tests/tls/SslContextManagerTest.java:164) to verify these provider-affecting options produce distinct cache entries while unchanged options still reuse the cache.
